name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنشَأَ لَكُمُ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرَ وَٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةَ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٨)
```

```english
It is He who made for you hearing, eyesight, and hearts. Little do you thank. (78)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى ذَرَأَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٧٩)
```

```english
It is He who created you on the earth, and you will be mustered toward Him. (79)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ ٱخْتِلَـٰفُ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
And it is He who gives life and brings death, and due to Him is the alternation of day and night. Do you not exercise your reason? (80)
```

```arabic
بَلْ قَالُوا۟ مِثْلَ مَا قَالَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٨١)
```

```english
Indeed, they say, just like what the former peoples said. (81)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
They said, ‘What, when we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected? (82)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا هَـٰذَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
Certainly, we and our fathers were promised this before. [But] these are nothing but myths of the ancients.’ (83)
```

```arabic
قُل لِّمَنِ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘To whom does the earth belong and whoever it contains, if you know?’ (84)
```

```arabic
سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٨٥)
```

```english
They will say, ‘To Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then take admonition?’ (85)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَن رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ ٱلسَّبْعِ وَرَبُّ ٱلْعَرْشِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٨٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘Who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the Great Throne?’ (86)
```

```arabic
سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ أَفَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (٨٧)
```

```english
They will say, ‘[They belong] to Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’ (87)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَنۢ بِيَدِهِۦ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلَا يُجَارُ عَلَيْهِ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (٨٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘In whose hand is the dominion of all things, and who gives shelter and no shelter can be provided from Him, if you know?’ (88)
```

```arabic
سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ ۚ قُلْ فَأَنَّىٰ تُسْحَرُونَ (٨٩)
```

```english
They will say, ‘[They all belong] to Allah.’ Say, ‘Then how are you so deluded?’ (89)
```

```arabic
بَلْ أَتَيْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكَـٰذِبُونَ (٩٠)
```

```english
Indeed, We have brought them the truth, and they are indeed liars. (90)
```

```arabic
مَا ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِن وَلَدٍ وَمَا كَانَ مَعَهُۥ مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ ۚ إِذًا لَّذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلَـٰهٍۭ بِمَا خَلَقَ وَلَعَلَا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ (٩١)
```

```english
Allah has not taken any offspring, neither is there any god besides Him, for then each god would take away what he created, and some of them would surely rise up against others. Clear is Allah of what they allege! (91)
```

```arabic
عَـٰلِمِ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ فَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٩٢)
```

```english
The Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is above having any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (92)
```

```arabic
قُل رَّبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّى مَا يُوعَدُونَ (٩٣)
```

```english
Say, ‘My Lord! If You should show me what they are promised, (93)
```

```arabic
رَبِّ فَلَا تَجْعَلْنِى فِى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٩٤)
```

```english
then do not put me, my Lord, among the wrongdoing lot.’ (94)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّرِيَكَ مَا نَعِدُهُمْ لَقَـٰدِرُونَ (٩٥)
```

```english
We are indeed able to show you what We promise them. (95)
```

```arabic
ٱدْفَعْ بِٱلَّتِى هِىَ أَحْسَنُ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ ۚ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَصِفُونَ (٩٦)
```

```english
Repel ill [conduct] with that which is the best. We know best whatever they allege. (96)
```

```arabic
وَقُل رَّبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزَٰتِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ (٩٧)
```

```english
Say, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection from the promptings of devils, (97)
```

```arabic
وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَن يَحْضُرُونِ (٩٨)
```

```english
and I seek Your protection, my Lord, from their presence near me.’ (98)
```

## (Commentary)

After He threatened them with a severe punishment from which there is no turning back and no escape, and refuted every excuse they could possibly offer, and clarified that the sole reason for their disbelief in Allah and the Last Day is the following of whims and the dislike of following the truth, He completed the exposition by establishing the proof for His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية) and for the return of creation to Him, by mentioning clear signs (آيات بينة) that leave no room for denial.

Following this, He commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to seek refuge in Him from being included in the punishment with which they were threatened, and to seek refuge in Him from the promptings of the devils (همزات الشيطان) and from their presence around him, as they had done with the disbelievers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنْشَأَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ قَلِيلاً ما تَشْكُرُونَ** — **"It is He who created for you hearing, sight, and hearts; little is the gratitude you show" (23:78).** The Glorified began the mention of the favors He has bestowed upon them by citing the origination of hearing and sight. These are two blessings specific to the animal kingdom, created through origination (إنشاء) and innovation (إبداع) without a prior model, as they are not found in the simple species that precede animals, such as plants, minerals, and the elements.

With the acquisition of these two senses, the existent equipped with them stands in a new position; the scope of its activity expands relative to those deprived of them to an immeasurable extent. It perceives its good and evil, its benefit and harm, and is granted, along with them, volitional movement (الحركة الإرادية) toward what it desires and away from what it dislikes. It settles in a fresh, new world containing manifestations of beauty, pleasure, glory, dominance, and love—things of which there was no report in what preceded it.

It is said that He restricted the mention of the senses to hearing and sight because logical deduction (الاستدلال) depends upon them and is completed through them.

Then the Glorified mentioned the heart (الفؤاد), referring to the principle of intellection (المبدأ الذي يعقل) in the human being. This is a blessing specific to humans among all other animals. The stage of acquiring the heart is a new existential stage, higher in degree, loftier in station, and wider in scope than the animal world, which is the world of the senses. Through it, the radius of the senses' operation first expands beyond what it was in general animals by an immeasurable degree; thus, the human being perceives through them what is absent and what is present, what has passed and what remains of the reports of things, their traces, and their descriptions, whether through direct engagement or otherwise.

Then he ascends by his heart—that is, by his intellection (تعقله)—to what is beyond the sensibles (المحسوسات) and the particulars (الجزئيات). He intuits universals (الكليات), thereby deriving universal laws (القوانين الكلية). He dives deep, reflecting upon theoretical sciences (العلوم النظرية) and true gnosis (المعارف الحقيقية), and penetrates through the authority of contemplation (سلطان التدبر) into the regions of the heavens and the earth.

In all of this, there is such wondrous divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) in the origination of hearing, sight, and hearts that man cannot possibly fulfill the gratitude due for it.

His saying: **قَلِيلاً ما تَشْكُرُونَ** (little is the gratitude you show) contains a degree of reproach. Its meaning is: "you show but a little gratitude." Thus, the word "little" (قَلِيلاً) is an adjective for the absolute object (المفعول المطلق) standing in its place.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ** — **"It is He who scattered you in the earth, and to Him you will be mustered" (23:79).** Al-Raghib said: "Scattering (الذرأ) is Allah's bringing forth what He initiated. It is said: 'Allah scattered the creation,' meaning He brought their individual persons into existence." He also said: "Mustering (الحشر) is the removal of a group from their place of residence and unsettling them from it toward war or the like." End quote.

The meaning is: Since He made you possessors of sense and intellect, He manifested your existence in the earth, attached to it, and then He shall gather you and return you to the meeting with Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ وَلَهُ اخْتِلافُ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ** — **"It is He who gives life and brings death, and to Him belongs the alternation of night and day. Do you not exercise your reason?" (23:80).** The meaning of the verse is clear. His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يُحْيِي وَيُمِيتُ** (It is He who gives life and brings death) follows, in terms of meaning, the preceding sentence. That is: since He made you possessors of knowledge and manifested your existence in the earth for a time until you are mustered to Him, this necessitated the law (سنة) of giving life and bringing death, for knowledge depends on life, and mustering depends on death.

His saying: **وَلَهُ اخْتِلافُ اللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ** (and to Him belongs the alternation of night and day) follows what preceded it, for life and then death are not completed except by the passage of time and the arrival of night after day and day after night, until the lifespan is exhausted and the written term (الأجل المكتوب) arrives. This is if the "alternation of night and day" refers to the arrival of one after the other. If it refers to their variation in length and shortness, it contains an allusion to the creation of the four seasons of the year, which branch out from the length and shortness of night and day. Through this, the matter of providing for animals and the governance of their livelihood is completed, as He said: **وَقَدَّرَ فِيها أَقْواتَها فِي أَرْبَعَةِ أَيَّامٍ سَواءً لِلسَّائِلِينَ** — "and ordained its foods in four days, alike for all seekers" (41:10).

Thus, the contents of the three verses are ordered and follow one another: the origination of hearing, sight, and the heart—which are the senses and the intellect for the human being—entails a life attached to matter and a dwelling in the earth for a time, then the return to Allah. This, in turn, entails life and death, which entails a lifespan that expires with the passage of time and a provision (رزق) by which one is sustained.

The three verses, therefore, include an allusion to a complete cycle of the governance of human affairs from the moment of creation until the return to the Lord. Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner of His creation; thus, He is the Owner of the governance of their affairs, because this governance is a creative governance (تدبير تكويني) that does not separate from creation and origination, nor is it distinct from it. It is the system of action and passion (نظام الفعل والانفعال) running between things through the various links established by creation (التكوين). Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is their Lord, the Governor of their affairs, and to Him they shall be mustered. His saying: **أَفَلا تَعْقِلُونَ** (Do you not exercise your reason?) is a rebuke to them and an exhortation to take heed and then believe.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلْ قالُوا مِثْلَ ما قالَ الْأَوَّلُونَ** — **"Rather, they said just what the ancients had said" (23:81).** This is a transition (إضراب) from a prior negation implied by the preceding interrogation; meaning: they did not exercise reason, but rather they said such and such.

In likening their speech to the speech of the ancients, there is an allusion to the fact that the imitation of their forefathers prevented them from following the truth and cast them into that which leaves no utility for religion—namely, the denial of the Resurrection (المعاد). Devotion to the earth and immersion in materialities is a prevailing custom (سنة جارية) among them, both the later and the earlier of them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ** — **"They said, ‘What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected?" (23:82).** This is an exposition of His saying: **قالُوا** (they said) in the previous verse, and the speech is based on deeming the matter improbable (استبعاد).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ وُعِدْنا نَحْنُ وَآباؤُنا هذا مِنْ قَبْلُ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** — **"We and our fathers were certainly promised this before; these are nothing but myths of the ancients" (23:83).** The term **myths (الأساطير)** refers to falsehoods and superstitious tales. It is the plural of *usturah* (أسطورة), just as *akadhib* (أكاذيب) is the plural of *ukdhubah* (falsehood) and *a'ajib* (أعاجيب) is the plural of *u'jubah* (wonder). The use of the plural "myths" for the Resurrection (البعث), which is singular, is with the consideration that it is a collection of promises, each of which is a myth—such as the giving of life, the gathering, the mustering, the reckoning, Paradise, Hell, and others. The word "this" (هذا) refers to the report of the Resurrection. His saying: **مِنْ قَبْلُ** (before) is connected to His saying: **وُعِدْنا** (we were promised), as indicated by the context of the sentence.

The meaning is: the promise of the Resurrection is an old promise, not a new one; we swear that we and our fathers were promised this before. This promised Resurrection is nothing but superstitious tales composed and organized by the people of old in the form of the revival of the dead, the reckoning of deeds, Paradise, Hell, reward, and punishment.

Their evidence for these being myths is that the prophets from ancient times have never ceased promising us and frightening us with the standing of the Hour, and had it been a truth and not a superstition, it would have occurred.

From here, it becomes clear, firstly, that their saying: **مِنْ قَبْلُ** (before) is to pave the way for the argument in their subsequent saying: **إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** (these are nothing but myths of the ancients).

Secondly, the speech is intended as an escalation (ترقي). The previous verse: **أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ** was based on deeming it improbable, while this verse contains a denial based on a flimsy argument.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لِمَنِ الْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — **"Say, ‘To whom does the earth belong and whoever is in it, if you know?’" (23:84).** When He mentioned their deeming the Resurrection improbable and then their denial of it, He began to argue for its possibility through the path of ownership (الملك), Lordship (الربوبية), and authority (السلطنة). He directed the speech to the idolaters who deny the Resurrection, while they acknowledge Him, the Exalted, in the sense that He is the Originator of the world and the Lord of lords, and that the deities worshipped besides Him are His creatures. Therefore, the existence of the Exalted was taken as a given within the argument.

Thus, His saying: **قُلْ لِمَنِ الْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيها** is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to ask them: who is the owner of the earth and those of intellect within it? It is well known that the question is about true ownership (الملك الحقيقي)—which is the subsistence of the existence of a thing through another thing such that the owned thing is not independent of its owner in any way—rather than conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري) which we, the members of society, have established for the benefit of social life, and which accepts validity and invalidity and becomes subject to buying and selling. This is because the speech is intended to prove the validity of all creative disposals (التصرفات التكوينية), and their criterion is true creative ownership, not conventional legislative ownership.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ أَفَلا تَذَكَّرُونَ** — **"They will say, ‘To Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then take admonition?’" (23:85).** This is an announcement of their answer, which is that the earth and whoever is in it are owned by Allah. They have no choice but to acknowledge that they belong to Allah, the Glorified, for this type of ownership only subsists through the originating cause (العلة الموجدة) for its effect (معلولها), where the existence of the effect subsists through it in a way that it is not independent of it in any respect. The originating cause of the earth and whoever is in it is Allah, the Glorified, alone without partner, even by the admission of the idolaters.

His saying: **قُلْ أَفَلا تذَكَّرُونَ** is a command, after recording the answer, to rebuke them for their failure to be reminded by the proof indicating the possibility of the Resurrection. The meaning is: say to them, if Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner of the earth and whoever is in it, why do you not remember that He has—by virtue of His ownership—the right to dispose of its inhabitants by giving them life after death?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ السَّبْعِ وَرَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ** — **"Say, ‘Who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the Great Throne?’" (23:86).** He commanded him a second time to ask them: who is the Lord of the seven heavens and the Lord of the Great Throne?

What is meant by the Throne (العرش) is the station where the reins of all affairs are gathered and from which every governance (تدبير) originates. The repetition of the word "Lord" (الرب) in His saying: **وَرَبُّ الْعَرْشِ الْعَظِيمِ** is to point to the importance of its matter and the loftiness of its station, just as Allah described it with greatness (العظمة). The discussion regarding it has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-A'raf in the eighth volume of this book.

They mentioned that our saying: "To whom belong the seven heavens?" and our saying: "Who is the Lord of the seven heavens?" are of the same meaning, just as one says: "To whom belongs the house?" and "Who is the lord of the house?" Thus, His saying: **مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ السَّبْعِ** is a question about their owner. Therefore, He recounted the answer from them with His saying: **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ** (They will say, "To Allah") based on the meaning. Had the answer been given as "Allah" (الله), as in the other recitation, it would have been an answer based on the literal wording.

However, it must be noted that what is established in the language is that the **Lord (رب)** of a thing is its owner who governs its affairs through disposal (التصرف). Thus, Lordship (الربوبية) is more specific than ownership (الملك). If "Lord" were synonymous with "Owner," the sequence of the answer following the question in the two previous verses—**قُلْ لِمَنِ الْأَرْضُ وَمَنْ فِيها** until His saying **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ**—would not be sound. For the meaning of the question would have been: "Who is the Lord of the earth and whoever is in it?" and it is well known that they used to affirm the lordship of their deities besides Allah over the earth and whoever is in it. Thus, their answer would have been to affirm lordship for their deities without being compelled to affirm it for Allah, the Glorified. This is in contrast to the question about the owner of the earth and whoever is in it, for the answer to it affirms it for Allah, because they viewed origination (الإيجاد) as belonging to Allah, and ownership is a necessary consequence of origination; thus, they were compelled to acknowledge it.

Furthermore, assuming that "Lord" is more specific than "Owner," one might imagine that a problem arises regarding the sequence of the answer following the question in the verse under discussion: **قُلْ مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ السَّبْعِ** until His saying **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ**. For the majority of the idolaters among the Sabians and others believe that the heavens and what is in them of the sun, moon, and others have deities besides Allah. Thus, if they were to answer the question about the Lord of the heavens, they would answer by affirming lordship for their deities besides Allah; so His saying: **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ** would not be sound, as there would be nothing compelling them to acknowledge Him.

What resolves the root of this problem is that a deep investigation into the beliefs of these people reveals that they did not build their views regarding the matter of deities upon a single principle or set of organized principles accepted by all. Groups like the Sabians, Brahmins, and Buddhists used to divide the affairs of the world into types and categories—such as the matter of the heaven, the earth, the types of animals, plants, land, sea, and so on—and they would affirm for each of these a deity besides Allah whom they worshipped instead of Allah, considering it an intercessor (شفيع) and a means of proximity (مقرب), and then they would take an idol to represent it.

As for their general masses among the uncivilized, such as the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance and those living on the fringes of the inhabited world, their beliefs in this regard were not built upon precise rules. Sometimes they would see deities besides Allah for the inhabited earth and its residents, having idols for them, and sometimes they would see the manufactured idols themselves as deities. As for the heavens and the celestial bodies, as well as the seas, they viewed them as being under the lordship of Allah, the Glorified, and Allah as their Lord. This is hinted at by the saying of Allah recounting the words of Pharaoh: **يا هامانُ ابْنِ لِي صَرْحاً لَعَلِّي أَبْلُغُ الْأَسْبابَ أَسْبابَ السَّماواتِ فَأَطَّلِعَ إِلى إِلهِ مُوسى** — "O Haman, build me a tower so that I may reach the routes, the routes of the heavens, and look upon the God of Moses" (40:36-37). Its apparent meaning is that he viewed the one to whom Moses called—namely, Allah the Exalted—as the God of the heaven. In short, the heavens and what is in them and who is in them of the angels were, in their view, under the lordship of Allah, the Glorified; then the angels were lords for what is below the heavens.

As for the Sabians and those who follow their path, they—as you have heard—view the heavens and what is in them of stars and planets as having deities and lords besides Allah, namely the angels and the jinn. They view the angels and the jinn as existents immaterial (مجردة عن المادة) and pure from the defilement of nature. When they consider them as inhabitants of the heavens, they mean the inner dimension of this world, which is the celestial, higher world (العالم السماوي العلوي) in which affairs are ordained, from which the decree (القضاء) descends, and by which natural causes are sustained. This world, with its angels and others, is under the lordship of Allah, the Glorified, even if those within it are deities for the sensory world and lords for those within it; and Allah is the Lord of lords.

Once this introduction is established, we say: if the address in the noble verse is directed to the Arab polytheists, as is apparent, then the question about the Lord of the seven heavens and the answer to it by their acknowledgment that it is Allah is appropriate, as you have seen.

If the address is directed to others who see a deity for the heaven besides Allah, then what is meant by the heaven is the celestial world with its inhabitants of angels and jinn, rather than the material heavens. This is supported by its being paired with the question about the Lord of the Great Throne, for the Throne is the station from which the commands related to the totality of creation originate—among whom are their lords and deities. It is well known that there is no lord for a station of this status except Allah, as nothing surpasses Him.

This higher world is, in their view, the world of lords and deities, and it has no lord except Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the question about its Lord and the answer to it by their acknowledgment that it is Allah is appropriate, as has been indicated.

So the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: Say, who is the Lord of the seven heavens from which the measures (أقدار) of affairs and their decrees descend, and the Lord of the Great Throne from which the commands originate for the totality of what is in the world, from the angels down to those below them? For they and what they possess are, according to your belief, owned by Allah, and He is the one who gave them ownership of what they possess.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ أَفَلا تَتَّقُونَ** — **"They will say, ‘To Allah.’ Say, ‘Will you not then be wary [of Him]?’" (23:87).** This is a recount of their answer by acknowledging that the seven heavens and the Great Throne belong to Allah, the Glorified.

The meaning is: they will answer you that they belong to Allah. Say to them by way of silencing and rebuking: if the seven heavens are that from which the command descends, and the Great Throne is that from which the command originates for Allah, the Glorified, then why do you not fear His wrath when you deny the Resurrection, considering it among the myths of the ancients, and mock His prophets who promised it to you? For it belongs to Him, the Exalted, to issue the command for the resurrection of the dead and the origination of the next life for the human being, and to send down the command for it from the heaven.

Among the subtle expressions of the verse is the use of the phrase **لِلَّهِ** (To Allah), for the proof is completed by ownership (الملك) even if they do not acknowledge Lordship (الربوبية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَنْ بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلا يُجارُ عَلَيْهِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — **"Say, ‘In whose hand is the dominion of all things, and who shelters, and against whom no shelter can be sought, if you know?’" (23:88).** Dominion (الملكوت) is sovereignty (الملك) in the sense of authority and rule, and it conveys an intensive meaning. The difference between *mulk* (sovereignty) and *malik* (owner) is that the owner is the one who owns the property, while the sovereign owns the owner and his property; thus, he has a sovereignty over another sovereignty and has the right of disposal by rule over the property and its owner.

The Exalted has explained His dominion (ملكوته) by His saying: **إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things" (36:82-83). Thus, the dominion of every thing is its being from His command, the Exalted, by the word "Be" (كن); in other words, its existence is from His origination (إيجاده), the Exalted.

So, the fact that the dominion of every thing is in His hand is a metaphorical allusion to the exclusivity of the origination of everything that can be called a "thing" to Him, the Exalted, as He said: **اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "Allah is the creator of all things" (39:62). Thus, His sovereignty, the Exalted, encompasses everything, and the penetration of His command and the execution of His rule are established over everything.

Since it might be imagined that the generality of sovereignty and the penetration of command do not preclude some of the causes and means He originated from interfering with His command, thereby doing to some of His creation what He does not intend or preventing Him from what He intends, He completed His saying: **بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** with His saying: **وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلا يُجارُ عَلَيْهِ** (and who shelters, and against whom no shelter can be sought). This is, in reality, a clarification of the exclusivity of sovereignty—that it is sovereignty in the full sense of the word. Nothing has any share of sovereignty alongside His sovereignty, even by way of prevention, interference, or objection. To Him belongs the sovereignty and to Him belongs the rule.

His saying: **وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلا يُجارُ عَلَيْهِ** is from *jiwar* (neighborliness), which originally means proximity of dwelling. Then they established a right for neighborliness, which is the protection (حماية) of the neighbor for his neighbor against one who intends evil toward him, due to the honor of the neighbor for the neighbor by the proximity of the house. From this, verbs were derived; it is said: "he sought his shelter (استجاره) and he sheltered him (أجاره)," meaning he asked him for protection and he protected him, i.e., he prevented from him the one who intended evil toward him.

This applies to all His acts, the Exalted. There is no thing that Allah singles out for a gift, whether in its initiation or its survival, but that He preserves it according to what He wills and to the extent He wills, without any preventer preventing Him. For the prevention of a preventer—if assumed—would only be by permission and will from Him; thus, it would not be a prevention of Him, the Exalted, but rather a prevention from Him and a limitation of one of His acts by another of His acts. There is no cause among the causes that performs an action but that He, the Exalted, has the right to dispose of it in a way it does not intend, because He is the one who gave it ownership of the action by His will; so He has the right to prevent it from it or from part of it.

So the meaning of His saying: **وَهُوَ يُجِيرُ وَلا يُجارُ عَلَيْهِ** is that He prevents evil from one whom it was intended for, and nothing prevents Him if He intends evil for something.

The meaning of the verse is: Say to these deniers of the Resurrection: Who is the one to whom the origination of everything—with its properties and effects—is exclusive, and who protects the one who seeks His shelter, while nothing is protected from Him if He intends evil for something? If you know.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَيَقُولُونَ لِلَّهِ قُلْ فَأَنَّى تُسْحَرُونَ** — **"They will say, ‘To Allah.’ Say, ‘Then how are you bewitched?’" (23:89).** It is said that what is meant by "bewitchment" (السحر) is that a thing is imagined to a person contrary to what it actually is; so it is a metaphor or an allusion.

The meaning is: they will answer you that the dominion belongs to Allah. Say to them by way of silencing and rebuking: until when will the truth be imagined to you as falsehood? If absolute sovereignty belongs to Allah, the Glorified, then He has the right to originate the next life and return the dead for reckoning and reward by a command He issues, which is His saying: "Be" (كُنْ).

Know that the three arguments, just as they prove the possibility of the Resurrection, also prove His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية). For true ownership (الملك الحقيقي) is inseparable from the permissibility of disposals (التصرفات), and the owner who disposes is the Lord (الرب).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلْ أَتَيْناهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ** — **"Rather, We have brought them the truth, and they are indeed liars" (23:90).** This is a transition from the negation understood from the arguments established in the previous verses. The meaning is: since the established arguments point to the Resurrection and they acknowledge their validity, then what Our messengers promised them is not false; rather, We have brought them the truth through the tongues of the messengers, and they are indeed liars in their claim of its falsehood and their denial of the Resurrection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَا اتَّخَذَ اللهُ مِنْ وَلَدٍ وَما كانَ مَعَهُ مِنْ إِلهٍ إِذاً لَذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلهٍ بِما خَلَقَ وَلَعَلا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ** — **"Allah has not taken any son, nor is there any god besides Him, for then each god would have taken away what he created, and some of them would surely have risen up against others..." (23:91).** The claim of a son was common among the idolaters, who considered the angels, or some of them, and some of the jinn, and some of the saintly humans as children of Allah, the Glorified. The Christians followed them in their saying: "The Messiah is the son of Allah." This type of birth and sonship is based on the son possessing something of the reality of divinity (اللاهوت) and its essence, and his separation from it by a kind of derivation (اشتقاق); thus, the one named "son" would be a god born from a god.

As for the claimed sonship by adoption (التبني)—which is taking the child of another as a son for honor or another purpose—it does not necessitate the son possessing anything of the reality of the father, such as the saying of the Jews: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones." A son in this sense is not intended here because the speech is intended to negate the plurality of deities, and this type of sonship does not entail divinity, even if naming and being named by it is forbidden.

So, what is meant by "taking a son" is the origination of something by way of partiality and derivation such that it possesses, in some way, something of the reality of the originator, not the naming of an existing thing as a son for some purpose, as some have mentioned.

The "son"—as you have seen—is a more specific instance in their view than the "god," for some of their deities are not sons in their view. Thus, His saying: **مَا اتَّخَذَ اللهُ مِنْ وَلَدٍ وَما كانَ مَعَهُ مِنْ إِلهٍ** is an escalation from the negation of the more specific to the negation of the more general. The word "any" (مِنْ) in both sentences is extra for emphasis.

His saying: **إِذاً لَذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلهٍ بِما خَلَقَ** (for then each god would have taken away what he created) is an argument for the negation of plurality by explaining its impossibility. For the plurality of deities cannot be conceived except by their being distinct in some way such that they do not unite in the meaning of their divinity and lordship. The meaning of the lordship of a god over a portion of the cosmos and one of its types is the delegation of governance (التدبير) within it to him such that he is independent in his affair without needing anything other than himself, even the one who delegated the affair to him. It is also clear that from two distinct things, only two distinct matters can emanate.

The consequence of this is that each of the deities would be independent in what pertains to his type of governance, and the link of unity and connection between the types of governances running in the world would be severed—such as the system running in the human world being severed from the systems running in the types of animals, plants, land, sea, plains, mountains, earth, heaven, and others, and each of them from every other. In this would be the corruption of the heavens and the earth and whatever is in them. The unity of the cosmic system, the harmony of its parts, and the connection of the governance running within it belies this.

This is what is meant by His saying: **إِذاً لَذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلهٍ بِما خَلَقَ**, meaning: some of the deities would have separated from others by the governance that emanates from them.

His saying: **وَلَعَلا بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ** (and some of them would surely have risen up against others) is another impossibility necessitated by the plurality of deities, from which another argument for negation is formed. Its explanation is that the governances running in the cosmos are different: some are horizontal governances (التدابير العرضية), such as the two governances running in the land and the sea, or the two governances running in water and fire; and some are vertical governances (التدابير الطولية), which are divided into a general, universal, governing (حاكم) governance and a specific, particular, governed (محكوم) governance—such as the governance of the terrestrial world and the governance of the plants within it, or the governance of the celestial world and the governance of one of the stars in the heaven, or the governance of the material world in its entirety and the governance of one of the material species.

Thus, some governance—namely, the general, universal governance—rises above others in the sense that if what is below it were severed from it, what is below it would be nullified because its subsistence is through what is above it. Just as if there were no terrestrial world or the governance that runs in it generally, there would be no human world nor the governance that runs in it specifically.

The consequence of this is that the god to whom a high type of governance pertains would be superior relative to the god to whom a governance below it, more specific, and lower was delegated. The superiority (استعلاء) of a god over a god is impossible.

This is not because the aforementioned superiority necessitates the god being overcome by another, or being deficient in his power, needing another for his completion, or being limited—and limitation leads to composition (التركيب), all of which are among the concomitants of possibility (الإمكان) that contradict the necessity of existence (وجوب وجود) for a god, thus leading to a contradiction—as the commentators have established. For the idolaters do not view their deities besides Allah as having necessity of existence; rather, they are, in their view, high possible existents to whom the governance of what is below them has been delegated. They are under the lordship of Allah, the Glorified, and are lords for what is below them, and Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of lords and the God of gods, and He alone is the Necessary Existent by Essence (الواجب الوجود بالذات).

Rather, the impossibility of superiority is because it necessitates the nullification of the independence of the one risen over in his governance and influence. For the dependence of governance on another and the need for him cannot coexist with independence. Thus, the lower of them would be seeking aid in his influence and needing the higher in it; so he would be one of the causes (الأسباب) through which the governance of what is below him is mediated, not a god independent in influence besides him. Thus, what was assumed to be a god would not be a god, but rather a cause by which the affair is governed—this is a contradiction.

This is what contemplation of the verse yields. The commentators have different paths in establishing the argument of the verse, all of which are built upon the plurality of deities necessitating matters that entail their possibility and contradict their being necessary existents, thus leading to a contradiction. Yet the people do not claim necessity of existence for any of their deities besides Allah. Some have gone to extremes, establishing the verse through ways composed of premises to which the verse has no allusion or hint. Others have fallen short, stating that the concomitance mentioned in the verse is customary (عادية), not intellectual (عقلية), and that the proof is persuasive (إقناعي), not definitive (قطعي).

Furthermore, do not be confused by His saying: **لَذَهَبَ كُلُّ إِلهٍ بِما خَلَقَ** where He attributed creation (الخلقة) to them, while it has preceded that they affirm a god of governance (تدبير) rather than origination (إيجاد). This is because some creation is part of governance, for the creation of a particular among particulars is that by whose existence the universal system of governance is completed relative to the running system. Thus, creation in the sense of action and governance are intertwined. Creation has been attributed to our actions as in His saying: **وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ وَما تَعْمَلُونَ** — "while Allah has created you and whatever you make" (37:96), and His saying: **وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما تَرْكَبُونَ** — "and made for you the ships and the cattle that you ride" (43:12).

So the people view each of the deities as a creator of what is below it—that is, an agent (فاعل) of it, just as one of us performs his actions. As for giving existence to things, that is something exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, alone; neither a monotheist nor an idolater doubts this, except for some among the theologians who did not distinguish between action and origination.

He concluded the verse with glorification by His saying: **سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ** — **"Immaculate is Allah from what they describe" (23:91).**

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عالِمِ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ فَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — **"Knower of the Unseen and the sensible; so He is exalted above the partners they ascribe [to Him]" (23:92).** This is an attribute for the Name of Majesty in His saying: **سُبْحانَ اللهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ**. Its being placed later is to indicate His knowledge of His own transcendence from their description of Him as having partners—as the context suggests. Thus, it is in the meaning of His saying: **قُلْ أَتُنَبِّئُونَ اللهَ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّماواتِ ولا فِي الْأَرْضِ سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — "Say, ‘Will you inform Allah of something He does not know in the heavens or the earth?’ Immaculate is He and exalted above the partners they ascribe [to Him]!" (10:18).

It returns, in reality, to an argument for the negation of partners through His testimony, the Exalted, that He does not know a partner for Himself, just as His saying: **شَهِدَ اللهُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "Allah bears witness that there is no god but Him" (3:18) is an argument by testimony for the negation of the root of existence [of other gods].

It is said that it is another proof returning to the establishment of loftiness (العلو) or the necessity of ignorance, which is a deficiency and the opposite of loftiness, because two multiple beings have no way for each to know the reality of the other as that other knows himself, by necessity; and this is a type of ignorance and limitation. End quote.

However, this, like the other proofs they have established, negates the plurality of the God who is the Necessary Existent by Essence, and the idolaters do not commit to that regarding their deities besides Allah. Moreover, some of the premises of the established proof are rejected.

His saying: **فَتَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** (so He is exalted above the partners they ascribe) is a derivation (تفريع) from all the preceding arguments for the negation of partners.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ رَبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّي ما يُوعَدُونَ رَبِّ فَلا تَجْعَلْنِي فِي الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ** — **"Say, ‘My Lord! If You should show me what they are promised, my Lord, then do not put me among the wrongdoing people’" (23:93-94).** When He finished recounting what they uttered of shirk (الشرك) against Allah, their denial of the Resurrection, and their mockery of the messengers, and established the arguments for the proof of their truth, He returned to the previous threat of punishment. He commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to ask Him to save him from the punishment with which He threatened them, if He were to show him that punishment.

So His saying: **قُلْ رَبِّ إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّي ما يُوعَدُونَ** is a command for supplication and seeking aid. The repetition of "Lord" (رَبِّ) is to emphasize the entreaty. The "ma" (ما) in His saying **إِمَّا تُرِيَنِّي** is extra, and it is what makes the entry of the *nun* of emphasis (نون التأكيد) upon the conditional particle correct; its root is *in turani* (إن ترني). In His saying: **ما يُوعَدُونَ** (what they are promised) there is an indication that some of the threats of punishment mentioned previously in the Surah are threats of worldly punishment. The phrase **رَبِّ فَلا تَجْعَلْنِي فِي الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ** (my Lord, then do not put me among the wrongdoing people) is a metaphorical expression for the punishment encompassing him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّا عَلى أَنْ نُرِيَكَ ما نَعِدُهُمْ لَقادِرُونَ** — **"We are indeed able to show you what We promise them" (23:95).** This is to comfort the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the power of his Lord to protect him while showing him the punishment He promised them. Perhaps what is meant by it is what Allah punished them with on the day of Badr, for Allah showed him that and showed it to the believers, and through it, He healed the rancor of their hearts.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ادْفَعْ بِالَّتِي هِيَ أَحْسَنُ السَّيِّئَةَ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَصِفُونَ** — **"Repel evil with what is best. We know best what they describe" (23:96).** That is: repel the evil that is directed toward you from them with a good deed, and choose for the repelling the best of good deeds. This is the repelling of evil with the good deed that is better; for example, if they treat you badly by causing harm, treat them well with the utmost of your ability, or with some degree of kindness. If you cannot do that, then by pardoning them.

His saying: **نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَصِفُونَ** (We know best what they describe) is a type of consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that what he encounters does not distress him, nor should what he witnesses of their audacity against their Lord grieve him, for He knows best what they describe.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقُلْ رَبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ مِنْ هَمَزاتِ الشَّياطِينِ وَأَعُوذُ بِكَ رَبِّ أَنْ يَحْضُرُونِ** — **"And say, ‘My Lord! I seek Your protection from the promptings of the devils, and I seek Your protection, my Lord, from their presence near me’" (23:97-98).** It is said in *Majma' al-Bayan*: "The *hamzah* (الهمزة) is the intensity of pushing. From it comes the *hamzah* for the letter that comes from the furthest part of the throat with strong reliance and pushing. The *hamzah* of the devil is his pushing through enticement (الإغواء) toward acts of disobedience." End quote. In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, narrated from him (peace be upon him): "It is the whispering (وسوسة) of the devils that falls into your heart."

In the two verses, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is commanded to seek refuge in his Lord from the enticement of the devils and from their presence near him. In this, there is an allusion that what the polytheists were afflicted with—of shirk and denial—was from the promptings of the devils and their surrounding them by their presence.
